hub_subtitle: Conversação para Viagens, Encontros Reais e Cultura

lesson.menu_title: Navigating Real Places -- Hotels, Airports and the Unexpected
lesson.menu_desc: Solving real travel problems politely with conditionals and polite requests -- 32 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 7 -- Navigating Real Places
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Marcelo de Azevedo Souza | Aula 7 -- Navigating Real Places

listenings.text: Hello Mr. Souza, this is Katrin, the duty manager at the front desk. I am so sorry about the mix-up with your reservation tonight. We were overbooked, and I know that is a real inconvenience after such a long trip. Here is what we can do. If you come down before noon tomorrow, we will move you into an adjoining suite at no extra cost, as an upgrade and a small apology from all of us. Breakfast will be complimentary for the rest of your stay, and I have added a little compensation to your account. If there is anything else you need tonight, just pick up the phone and dial zero. Again, I am sorry, and I hope the rest of your stay in Iceland is wonderful. Sleep well.

Welcome back to the travel show. Today, the art of complaining without losing your cool. Here is the secret most people miss. When something goes wrong at a hotel or an airport, the person in front of you is almost never the one who caused it. So do not attack them. Bring them onto your side. Start soft. Say, there seems to be a misunderstanding, I was told that my room was confirmed. Then make it easy for them to say yes. Is there any chance you could check availability for me? I would really appreciate it. Notice the pattern. You are calm, you are specific, and you give the other person a way to help you. The traveler who stays polite and firm almost always walks away with the upgrade. The one who shouts walks away with nothing. So next time your connecting flight is delayed and your bag is lost, take a breath, and ask for exactly what you need.
